coredata.dc:description: Herein, a mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSN) based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor (MSN-BA-SiCD) with improved performance was designed for monitoring hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Specifically, H2O2 response molecule (BA) with AIE feature was synthesized and encapsulated into MSN channel as the fluoresent recognition site. Afterwards, silane-modified carbon dot (SiCD) was covalently connected to the MSN surface which can effectively prevent the leakage of BA and improve the water dispersibility of the nanosensor. The presence of H2O2 could result in a “turn-on” fluorescence of BA as well as concurrent variation in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) signal between SiCD and BA. MSN-BA-SiCD displays a specific blue-to-green resolved emission change in response to H2O2 and the detection limit can be as low as 4.78 × 10−6 M. In addition, the nanosensor exhibited enhanced photostability due to the protection of the material framework. Moreover, the nanosensing system was successfully applied to detect H2O2 in beer, orange juice and living cells. The excellent water dispersibility and photostability of the nanosensor, together with high specificity and biocompatibility, constituted an advanced set of characteristics among existing MSNs, which was expected to further promote the development of H2O2 sensors in the environmental and biological fields.

head.ce:title: High dispersibility ratiometric fluorescence sensor designed by functionalized mesoporous silica nanopraticles for sensing and imaging of hydrogen peroxide

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSN) based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor (MSN-BA-SiCD) with improved performance was designed for monitoring hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Specifically, H2O2 response molecule (BA) with AIE feature was synthesized and encapsulated into MSN channel as the fluoresent recognition site. Afterwards, silane-modified carbon dot (SiCD) was covalently connected to the MSN surface which can effectively prevent the leakage of BA and improve the water dispersibility of the nanosensor. The presence of H2O2 could result in a “turn-on” fluorescence of BA as well as concurrent variation in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) signal between SiCD and BA. MSN-BA-SiCD displays a specific blue-to-green resolved emission change in response to H2O2 and the detection limit can be as low as 4.78 × 10−6 M. In addition, the nanosensor exhibited enhanced photostability due to the protection of the material framework. Moreover, the nanosensing system was successfully applied to detect H2O2 in beer, orange juice and living cells. The excellent water dispersibility and photostability of the nanosensor, together with high specificity and biocompatibility, constituted an advanced set of characteristics among existing MSNs, which was expected to further promote the development of H2O2 sensors in the environmental and biological fields.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous silica based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor (MSN-BA-SiCD) with improved performance is constructed for selective detection of H2O2 by a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Additionally, MSN-BA-SiCD has successfully achieved the detection of H2O2 in beverages and living cells, which indicates its possibility of further applications in sensing and bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a general name that refers to a series of derivatives of molecular oxygen in the process of biological life, which is very important functional signal molecule for physiology.[1,2] Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as a significant ROS in redox regulation of biological activities is very vital to many physiological processes. [3] The imbalance of H2O2 could cause deleterious effects to cellular structures, which would lead to the promotion of various diseases, such as cardiovascular disease,[4] neurodegenerative disease,[5] diabetes[6]and cancer.[7,8] Hence, establishing an accurate method for measuring the level of H2O2 is great prominence for health, environment and safety.[9,10] Compared with traditional detection techniques such as, liquid chromatography,[11] chemical titration,[12] potential and amperometric method,[13,14] the development of H2O2 fluorescence sensors is advantageous as they are convenient operation, low cost, high sensitivity and easy to realize point-of-care detection.[15–19] However, most of the fluorescence probes of H2O2 are based on single emission fluorescent response, and the recognition of the target is achieved through the intensity of the emission peak, which makes it easily affected by the environment and is unsuitable for naked eye imaging identification.[20–22] Recently, an attractive strategy to overcome these shortcomings is the dual-emission ratio fluorescence techniques that can monitor the target by ratio changes of the two emission intensities and self-eliminate the environmental interference.[23–25] At present, various types of nanomaterials have been explored in H2O2 ratio sensors, including quantum dots, gold nanoclusters, polymer nanoparticles, mesoporous silica and so on.[26–28].

Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) are considered as perspective fluorescence sensing platforms due to their defined mesoporous structure, high optical stability, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity.[29–31] Especially, the rich pore channel space and easy modified surface ensure the multiple modifications of fluorescent functional groups in MSNs, making them become excellent candidates for constructing ratio fluorescent sensors.[32] A series of MSNs based ratio fluorescent nanosensor have been developed during the past years and various functional groups (such as dye, carbon dots) were employed into the silica matrix to establish the dual-emission fluorescence.[33–35] Liu’s group has previously coated with silane carbon dots onto the surface of rhodamine B-doped silica to construct a dual-emission nanosensor, implementing intracellular Cu2+ sensing. The CDs with Cu2+ recognition units were covalently linked to Rhodamine B-doped silica nanoparticles through silylation reaction without any other surface functionalization, promising CD-functionlized MSN as an outstanding platform for biological applications.[36] Although MSNs show numerous advantages in optical sensing, they always suffer from the defects of effortless aggregation and poor dispersibility in water phase, which remain limit for their practical applications. In addition, the aggregation and the solid-state framework of MSNs always lead to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of the introducing fluorescent groups. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescent molecules, that show robust enhancement of fluorescence emission in aggregated state, open up a new sight for the development of fluorescence sensing technology in solid state materials. [37,38] Fluorescence nanomaterials by integrating AIEgens into MSNs have been investigated recently, which exhibit fascinating optical properties and ultra-bright fluorescence. We have previously introduced AIEgen into MSNs to construct a multifunctional ratio fluorescent probe, achieving intracellular sensing and hypoxia treatment.[39] Surface modification can bring in additional advantages, providing opportunities to enhanced water dispersion or biocompatibility of MSNs.[40–42] However, the most introduced fluorescent units in MSNs based ratio fluorescent probe are aimed to provide optical signals only and the improvement for their surface properties are rare study. Therefore, the brilliant design of the introduction of functional groups, which could provide the optical properties and surface improvement functions simultaneously, has become valuable strategy for the construction of ratio fluorescence sensor.

Based on the above, we designed a MSN based ratiometric fluorescence sensor (MSN-BA-SiCD) for H2O2 using an AIE featured recognition molecular as fluorescence response site and carbon dot as the fluorescence reference group. As shown in Scheme 1, the H2O2 responsive molecule (BA) that contain phenylboronic acid pinacol ester unit was synthesized firstly and loaded into the mesoporous channels of MSN. Meanwhile, the blue-emitted carbon dot (CD) with fine water dispersion was designed and covalent coated on the surface of MSN, which could effectively improve the water solubility of the material while serving as reference fluorescence. In the existence of H2O2, an intense green fluorescence detection signal appeared because of the peculiar reaction between BA unit and H2O2. Triggered by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect from CD to BA, a blue-to-green resolved ratiometric emission in response to H2O2 has been realized. The proposed MSN-BA-SiCD nanosensor showed linear ranges from 0 and 350 μM with a detection limit of 4.78 μM. Accordingly, MSN-BA-SiCD was able to detect intracellular H2O2 and achieved the color recognized imaging through laser scanning confocal microscopy (CLSM). The obtained MSNs are readily dispersed in water due to the modification of CD which could enhance water solubility and prevent aggregation. Owing to the protection of material framework and modification of surface, MSN-BA-SiCD exhibited improved photostability and biocompatibility. Moreover, these high stable ratio sensing platform was successfully expanded in the detection of H2O2 in diverse commercial drink samples. These results indicate that this ratio fluorescence nanosensor with facile method, stable optical performance and good biocompatibility has great potential in practical applications such as biosensing, environment detection and food analysis.

Tetraethoxy orthosilane (TEOS) and H2O2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). We purchased dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), citric acid, urea, 4-(diethylamino)salicylaldehyde, (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane ( APTES), tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (∼25% in H2O), cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50 mmol/L, PH=7.4) from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Company. Ethanol, dichloromethane (DCM), ethyl acetate and n-Hexane were bought from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Company. Benzothiazole-2-acetonitrile was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Company. 4-(Bromomethyl)benzeneboronicacidpinacolester was purchased from Energy Chemical ( Shanghai, China). K2CO3 was bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Company. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), NaNO3, sodium nitroferricyanide dehydrate, sodium hypochlorite solution (6–14% active chlorine basis), Xanthine, Xanthine Oxidase, 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionamidine) dihydrochloride(AAPH) and 2-Benzothiazoleacetonitrile were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Company.

According to the literature,[43] 1.93 g of 4-(Diethylamino)− 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde, 2.97 g of 4-(Bromomethyl)benzeneboronic acid pinacol ester and 1.36 g of K2CO3 were added in a three-necked flask with 20 mL tetrahydrofuran solution. The mixture was heated at 66 ℃ for 18 h under N2. Then, the mixture was purified by the silica column (petroleum benzin / ethyl acetate = 5 / 1). 4-(diethylamino)− 2-((4-(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolan-2-yl)benzyl)oxy)benzaldehyde (CB) was afforded as light pink solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 10.25 (s, 1 H), 7.82 (t, J = 8.0 Hz, 3 H), 7.73 (d, J = 8.9 Hz, 1 H), 7.45 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 2 H), 6.28 (d, J = 9.0 Hz, 1 H), 6.05 (s, 1 H), 5.19 (s, 2 H), 3.36 (q, J = 7.1 Hz, 4 H), 1.35 (s, 12 H), 1.15 (t, J = 7.1 Hz, 6 H)(Fig. S1).

200 mg of compounds CB, 100 mg of 2-benzothiazol acetonitrile and 5 drop tetrabutylammonium hydroxide solution were added in a flask with 10 mL ethanol to form a brown solution. Then, the above mixture was refluxed at 78 ℃ for 6 h under N2 and chilled to room temperature. The sediment was washed by cold ethanol and dried to acquire compounds BA as orange powerds. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO) δ 8.51 (s, 1 H), 8.27 (d, J = 9.2 Hz, 1 H), 8.10 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1 H), 7.96 (d, J = 8.1 Hz, 1 H), 7.75 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 2 H), 7.55 – 7.50 (m, 3 H), 7.42 (t, J = 7.6 Hz, 1 H), 6.55 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 1 H), 6.31 (s, 1 H), 5.38 (s, 2 H), 3.46 (q, J = 6.7 Hz, 4 H), 1.30 (s, 12 H), 1.10 (t, J = 7.0 Hz, 6 H)(Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of surface siloxane modified CD
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea were dissolved into 20 mL deionized water to get a transparent solution. The above mixture was heated at 160 ℃ for 4 h in the Teflon-line actoclave. After the reaction cooled to the room temperature, the reaction solution was mixed with 40 mL ethanol and the deposition was collected by centrifugation (10 min at 8000 r / min). The deposition was washed with ethanol for two times and then dried in vacuo to attain CD.[44].

200 mg of EDC, 200 mg of NHS and 200 mg of CD were dissolved in 2 mL ethanol and whipped for 6 h at 25 ℃ for activation. Then, 150 μL of APTES was added to the above mixed solution and whipped for 12 h at 25 ℃. Finally, the sediment was washed with ethanol for three times to obtain silane modified CD (SiCD), and then the SiCD was dispersed with 4 mL of ethanol by ultrasound.

0.2 g of CTAB and 32 mL of deionized water were added in a three-necked flask. 1.4 mL of NH3·H2O solution was added to above solution and whipped for 30 min at 35 ℃. Then, the mixture of TEOS (1 mL) and n-hexane (0.4 mL) was added slowly to the above solution drop by drop. After agitating for 12 h, the white sediment acquired by filtrating was washed with ethanol and dried at room temperature. Finally, MSN was acquired by calcination in a muffle furnace at 500 ℃ for 5 h to remove the surfactant.
The BA loading was obtained by ultrasonic evaporation. 10 mg of BA was dissolved in DCM. 50 mg of MSN was added into above solution. Then, the dried materials (MSN-BA) was acquired by ultrasound.

10 mg of above MSN-BA material was dispersed in 3 mL of ethanol, 100 μL of NH3·H2O solution was added to above solution. After whipping for 30 min, 150 μL of SiCD solution was added and whipped for 15 h at room temperature. Finally, the sediment was collected and washed with water and ethanol respectively. The obtained material was named as MSN-BA-SiCD.
For comparison sample MSN-SiCD, the synthetic method was the same as MSN-BA-SiCD, except that MSN-BA was replaced by MSN.

For fluorescence sensing tests, different concentration of H2O2 was added into of MSN-BA-SiCD solution and the MSN-BA-SiCD solution was diluted to 5 × 10−5 g/mL with PBS buffer (50 mM, pH=7.4). The fluorescence spectra were collected after 2 h.

For the selective experiment, the concentration of the reference analytes were 4.5 × 10−4 M, the concentration of MSN-BA-SiCD was 5 × 10−5 g/mL, and the fluorescence spectra were collected 2 h later. In the interference experiments, the concentration of H2O2 and interference analytes were 4.5 × 10−4 M and the concentration of MSN-BA-SiCD was 0.05 mg/mL. The fluorescence spectra were collected 2 h later.

For the photostability tests, BA (10−5 M) and MSN-BA-SiCD (5 ×10−5 g/mL) solutions were respectively placed in a quartz pool with an optical path length of 1 cm. After continuous ultraviolet irradiation (λex=365 nm), the fluorescence emission intensity of BA and MSN-BA-SiCD was measured every 5 min for continuous irradiation for 45 min.

Log P values for BA, MSN and MSN-BA-SiCD were measured by “shaking flask” method according to the literature.[45] Octanol and deionized water were mixed and agitated for 24 h to acquire octanol-saturated water and water-saturated octanol. After reaching equilibrium, two phases were segregated. BA(400 μL, 8 mg/mL, water-saturated octanol), MSN (400 μL, 5 mg/mL, water-saturated octanol) or MSN-BA-SiCD (450 μL, 5 mg/mL, water-saturated octanol) was added to the mixture of 4 mL octanol-saturated water and 3.6 mL water-saturated octanol respectively, and the melange was shaken on an orbital shaker for 24 h under 25 ± 1 ℃. The melange was enabled to stewing 3 h, and then separate BA, MSN and MSN-BA-SiCD from two phases. The contents of MSN and MSN-BA-SiCD in octanol and water were measured by ICP-MS respectively. The contents of BA in octanol and water were measured by fluorescence measurements respectively. The Log P values can be acquired from:
Log P =

Beer and orange juice samples were gained from the local market and first pretreated with pH buffer to adjust the pH to 7.4. Then, the MSN-BA-SiCD was added to these drink samples and diluted to 5 × 10−5 g/mL by DMSO/PBS buffer (1:99 v/v)·H2O2 of diverse concentrations were added to the mixed suspension for fluorescence spectrum measurement (λex=365 nm), which could evaluate the concentrations with the fluorescence alteration indued by the adjunction of H2O2 and then compare it with the given values. Three replicates of each concentration were prepared in parallel.

MTT assay was used to detect the effect of MSN-BA-SiCD on cell viability. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HepG2) cells were inoculated into 96-well cell culture plates with a density of 1 × 105 cells /mL. After 24 h of cultivation, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 80 μg/mL of MSN-BA-SiCD were added for cultured 24 h, and then 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After 4 h of cultivation, the medium was sucked out and 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added. The optical density (OD) of each hole was measured on the metric microplate at the wavelength of 490 nm.

HepG2 cells were cultivated in RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (v/v) and in an incubator at 36 ℃ with 5% CO2 and 95% air. For the fluorescence imaging experiment, HepG2 cells were inoculated into confocal petri dishes with a density of 1 × 104 cells /mL. After 24 h of culture, the concentration of 50 µg/mL MSN-BA-SiCD was added for 8 h. Then, 100 µM H2O2 was added into the culture dish and incubated for 0 h, 1 h, 2 h, and 4 h, respectively. After washing with PBS buffer, living cell imaging experiments were made with CLSM under λex = 410 nm respectively.

In this study, a unique MSN based nano-composites containing fluorescent H2O2 recognition molecule (BA) and silanized carbon dot (SiCD) were prepared via a simple dual-functionalization strategy combining pore loading and surface modification. Firstly, fragment CB with phenyl borate ester group was prepared by 4-(Diethylamino)− 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde and 4-(Bromomethyl)benzeneboronic acid pinacol ester, and then BA was synthesized using an condensation reaction by fragment CB and 2-Benzothiazol acetonitrile (Fig. S3). After removing the template of MSNs, BA with AIE characteristic was loaded into the pore channel of MSNs to provide a recognition site for H2O2. Meanwhile, the water soluble CD with blue fluorescence was further modified with the siloxane to obtain SiCD (Fig. S4). Then, SiCD was covalently attached onto the surface of MSNs, which could effectively prevent the leakage of BA and improved the water dispersibility of the material (Scheme 1). Evidence of the successful synthesis and functionalization of materials was confirmed through the FT-IR measurements. The silylation modification of CD was first demonstrated. As shown in Fig. S5, the characteristic peaks of νN-H (3392 cm−1), νC
O (1676 cm−1) and δN-H (1649 cm−1) for CONH group could be observed for SiCD. Meanwhile, compared with the curve of CD, SiCD exhibit the reduced peak at 3400 cm−1-2500 cm−1 which is corresponded to the νOH from COOH group. Besides, the characteristic peak of νSi-O (1082 cm−1) for silicon oxygen bond, νSi-C (1240 cm−1) for silane group and νC-H (2977 cm−1) for ethoxy group appear in the spectrum of SiCD, indicating the successful modification of siloxane on CD. [61,62] Compared with MSN, two new characteristic peaks of BA are observed in the spectra of MSN-BA, the νC-H (2979 cm−1) of the CC groups and the νC
C (1610 cm−1-1510 cm−1) of the biphenyl rings, which.

illustrates the loading of BA ( Fig. 1a). In addition, the characteristic peak of SiCD (νC
O at 1676 cm−1) appears in the spectrum of MSN-BA-SiCD, which demonstrates the successful connection of SiCD on the MSN-BA. The covalent interaction between SiCD and the surface of MSN is confirmed by solid-state 29Si NMR spectra (Fig. S6). Compared with the MSN, the spectrum of MSN-SiCD display two regions of major peak, centered at about − 85 to − 118 and − 45 to − 75, which attributes to Si(−O−)4 (Qn sites) species and RSi(−O−)3 (Tn sites), respectively. Furthermore, there is no detection of uncondensed silicon species, indicating that the SiCD is attached on the surface of MSN. Moreover, the changes in zeta potential among MSN (−17.5 ± 0.6 mV), MSN-BA (−19.4 ± 0.6 mV), and MSN-BA-SiCD (21.9 ± 1.0 mV) also suggest the stepwise modification of MSN. (Fig. S7). [46] The zeta potential was change to positive charge after the attachment of SiCD, which might be ascribed to the presence of positively charged amide groups in SiCD.[59,60] And this phenomenon was already reported in several studies of carbon dot modified MSN by covalent linkage.[63,64].

The small-angle x-ray scattering (SAXS) results of MSN, MSN-BA and MSN-BA-SiCD are given in Fig. 1b. The SAXS result of MSN shows a sharp scattering peak at q= 1.40 nm, indicating the formation of ordered mesoporous structure in it. In addition, four peaks of MSN at 1.40, 2.38, 2.70 and 3.80 nm−1 correspond to the (100), (110) (200) and (210) crystal planes of 2D-hexagonal symmetry, respectively. The lattice parameter is 5.18 nm which is calculated formula equal to
2 d /
3
.Likewise, there are similar peaks on the curve of MSN-BA and MSN-BA-SiCD, demonstrating that the orderliness of the mesoporous structure is not changed by the loaded BA and modified SiCD. Both at once, no obvious diffraction peak of organic molecule BA is detected, indicating that BA exists in an amorphous form in mesoporous channels.[47] As we can see from Fig. 1c, The N2 adsorption/desorption curves of MSN, MSN-BA and MSN-BA-SiCD belong to the typical IV isotherm, suggesting the presence of well-defined mesoporous structures. Through the Barrett−Joyner−Halenda (BJH) method, the pore size distribution was acquired around 2 nm. The BET surface area and total pore volume of MSN are calculated to be 993.7 m2/g and 1.0 m3/g, respectively. However, the specific surface area and total pore volume of the prepared MSN-BA are decreased, indicating the successful loading of BA. Similarly, the specific surface area and total pore volume of MSN-BA-SiCD are further reduced, which could be owed to the successful modification of SiCD (Table S1).

Further investigation of the morphology and structure of MSN-BA and MSN-BA-SiCD samples was conducted by TEM and SEM. Recognizable nanospheres with an average diameter around 201 nm could be seen from the SEM vision of MSN-BA-SiCD ( Fig. 2a). Compared with the SEM images of MSN and MSN-BA, the morphology of the nanosensor have not been affected by the subsequent loading and surface modification (Fig. S8). The 2D-hexagonal mesoporous structures are observed in the TEM images of MSN-BA and MSN-BA-SiCD (Fig. 2b, c). The center distance of the mesochannels of MSN-BA-SiCD was about 4.44 nm, which is consistent with the results of SAXS. In addition, the modification of SiCD on MSN surface was characterized using TEM images. As we can see from Fig. S9, the signature lattice of as-prepared CD is about 0.21 nm. After functionalization with SiCD, well-defined 0.21 nm lattice of CD is discovered on the MSN surface, proving the successful graft of SiCD on MSN (Fig. 2c).[44] Furthermore, the existence of Si and N in these MSN based materials is confirmed by the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). N elemental that can only be derived from CD is well matched on the surface of MSN-SiCD, which could ulteriorly testify the effective fixation of SiCD (Fig. 2d∼h).

The successful modification of CD on the MSN surface can effectively improve the water dispersibility of the material due to the excellent water solubility of the CD. The hydration particle sizes of MSN, MSN-BA, MSN-BA-SiCD in aqueous solution were measured by dynamic light scatterer (DLS). As shown in Fig. 1d, the wide particle size distribution are displayed for MSN and MSN-BA, indicating the existence of varying degrees of aggregation. And the average particle size of them could reaches 327 nm and 530 nm, respectively. Whereas, MSN-BA-SiCD has apparent narrow particle size distribution and smaller average particle size compared with MSN and MSN-BA. The average particle size of MSN-BA-SiCD decreased to 216 nm, which is consistent with the average diameter of nanomaterials in SEM image. Therefore, the modification of CD can effectively improve the water dispersibility of materials and prevent aggregation. The oil-water partition coefficient log P is a measure of molecular lipophilicity or hydrophobicity,[48]which can provide direct information of the hydrophilic properties and be used to predict water solubility and toxicity.[49] The results obtained by shaking bottle method show that the log P of BA and MSN are 1.62 and − 0.01, respectively. Poor water solubility is displayed for BA sensing molecules, which is consistent with the results of DLS. However, the log P of MSN-BA-SiCD decreased to − 0.06, indicating the improvement of the water solubility for MSN-BA-SiCD due to the functionalization of CD. [50–52] In order to further evaluate the surface hydrophilic properties of the material, contact angle (CA) experiment is used to test the wettability of the material in solid state.[53] The materials are prepared into thin film by droplet coating and ensures that the droplets completely drop onto the film of materials without touching the substrate. The CA of BA is 92.1°, suggesting that it is hydrophobic. On the contrary, the water droplet rapidly diffused to the solid surface of SiCD within seconds, which indicates the strong hydrophilicity for SiCD. The CA of MSN and MSN-BA-SiCD are 28.3° and 17.6°, respectively. The decrease in CA further proves the hydrophilicity enhancement caused by SiCD modification for the nanosensor.

In this ratio fluorescence sensing platform, BA and SiCD are used as sensing recognition sites and internal standards, respectively. The optical properties were investigated by UV–vis spectra firstly ( Fig. 3a), the spectrum of SiCD exhibited an absorption band at 338 nm, which is attributable to its SP-conjugated structure. [44]A broad band around 450–550 nm corresponds to BA. Both of the absorption bands of SiCD and BA are found in the spectrum of MSN-BA-SiCD, which further indicates their successful modification. As we can see from the fluorescence measurement (Fig. S10), BA has weak fluorescence emission at around 625 nm in the absence of H2O2, while SiCD has strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm. Therefore, only a significant fluorescence emission at 450 nm is shown for as-prepared MSN-BA-SiCD.

After interaction with H2O2, BA exhibits strong green fluorescence through a specific cyclization reaction, which could be tested by the fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). At the presence of H2O2, BA displays a remarkable enhancement (about 27 times) of fluorescence intensity around 520 nm. Phenyl borate ester group of BA could be oxidized and then formed highly conjugated compounds during this process.[43] Concurrently, linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and the concentration of H2O2 from 0 to 4.0 × 10−4 M is established (R2 =0.9862) (Fig. 3b insert). Additionally, the absorption spectrum of BA after reacted with H2O2 and the emission spectrum of SiCD are overlapped in the range of 400–500 nm, which deduces that effective FRET could be taken place from SiCD to BA (Fig. 3c).

The specific interaction of BA with H2O2 is demonstrated through 1H NMR and MS. After adding H2O2, borate group of BA can be oxidized by H2O2 and the C–O bond will be cleaved, which could trigger a cyclization reactions involving cyanide groups and give larger conjugated compound BA’ (Fig. 3d). The molecular weight of BA’ is calculated to be [M+H]+ 349.5. A significant signal is found at 351.1 in MS data for the sample of BA after reaction with H2O2, which is consistent with the calculated result (Fig. 3d). As shown in 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S11), the characteristic proton signal for NH group (i at 9.55 ppm) is detected after the reaction with H2O2. In addition, the ii proton signal on the newly formed hexahydric ring also appeared at 8.46 ppm, which further proves the generation of larger conjugated compound. Moreover, the appearance of larger conjugated structures often leads to changes in the absorption spectrum. A distinct increase absorption in 400–500 nm is observed after the addition of H2O2 for MSN-BA-SiCD.[43].

Interestingly, BA and BA’ exhibited attractive AIE properties. The fluorescence intensity of BA was almost negligible when water volume fraction (Fw) was less than 60% because of the speedy non-radiative decay of the singlet excited state, which was triggered by the intramolecular rotation of CC bond in the alkene linker.[54] The fluorescence intensity of BA increased distinctly as the Fw increased to 70%. The aggregation caused by water inhibited the cis-trans isomerism of CC bond in the olefin linker and therefore facilitated the emission. (Fig. S12) Obviously, BA showed a typical AIE characteristic in the aggregate state. Similarly, H2O2 oxidized BA and underwent cyclization, limiting the rotation of the CC bond and resulting in the formation of highly fluorescent BA’.[55] The AIE properties of BA’ had also been confirmed in water/THF mixtures. With the addition of water, the fluorescence intensity continuously increased. When Fw was 60%, BA’ exhibited the maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S13). The excellent fluorescence performance of BA provided an effective guarantee for its application in MSN solid materials.

For this ratiometric MSN-BA-SiCD sensor, FRET feature could be performed in the H2O2 sensing process, in which the SiCD was connected on the surface of MSNs as donor, and BA was loaded into mesoporous channel as receptor. The as-prepared MSN-BA-SiCD displays blue fluorescence emission of SiCD. However, in the presence of H2O2, strong green fluorescence could be emitted by BA through the cyclization reaction with H2O2. During the FRET process, the blue fluorescence of SiCD decreases gradually and the green fluorescence of BA increases constantly with the rise of H2O2, which will lead to a color changed ratio fluorescence sensing ( Fig. 4a). Meanwhile, the emergence of the FRET process that generated when fluorescence donor and receptor are close enough (distance less than 10 nm), further proves the successful modification of BA and SiCD in this nanosensor. As we can see from the Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of MSN-BA-SiCD at 516 nm increases continuously with the increasing content of H2O2 and the peak ratio at σ 516 nm / 450 nm changes significantly. Correspondingly, the color of its solution changes from blue to green (Fig. 4a insert and Fig. S14). In the process, the blue- emitted fluorescence of SiCD is switched off via FRET, exhibiting the green-emitted fluorescence of BA. Good linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and the concentration of H2O2 from 0 to 3.5 × 10−4 M is established (R2 =0.9879). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 4.78 μM using the equation of 3S0/k (S0 is the standard deviation and k is the slope), indicating the MSN-BA-SiCD have high sensitivity to H2O2 sensing.

As is well known, specificity is a vital indicator to survey the function of fluorescent probes.[56] Therefore, the fluorescent responses of the nanosensors to different ROS and other interfering ions were tested, including ROO', NO, O2 ·-, 1O2,·OH,·Bu, TBHP, HClO/ClO-,Fe3+, Fe2+, CO3 2-, HCO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO3 -. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the fluorescent intensity of MSN-BA-SiCD towards H2O2 is enhanced significantly and no obvious changes are observed for other ROS and interfering ions when different ions above were added to the MSN-BA-SiCD solution. Correspondingly, the color of the MSN-BA-SiCD solution have not evident variation when other ions were added (Fig. S15). The high selectivity of MSN-BA-SiCD towards H2O2 is caused by the specific reaction between H2O2 and the BA unite in the nanosensor. In addition, almost the same fluorescent intensity after addition of other interfering species together with H2O2 is found for the sample containing H2O2 only (Fig. 4e), which indicates that the MSN-BA-SiCD has excellent anti-interference ability for H2O2 sensing in aqueous solution..

The excellent optical stability is always an advantage of mesoporous nanoprobes due to the material network. The Fig. 4f shows the emission intensity of BA and MSN-BA-SiCD under continuous UV irradiation. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of BA decreases by 52.9% under excitation, while MSN-BA-SiCD only decreases by 18.3% within 45 min. Compared with organic molecules, the high stability of the nanoprobe may be attributed to the protective effect of the silica skeleton which could reduce external affecting to the organic molecules in the pore.[32,57,58].

In order to investigate the potential applications of MSN-BA-SiCD nanosensor in biosensing and biomedicine, the research of H2O2 identification forlive cell imaging is extended studied in biological systems. Specifically, HepG2 cells were incubated with MSN-BA-SiCD for 8 h and then 100 μM of H2O2 was added to co-incubate with the cells for 1 h, 2 h and 4 h respectively. As shown in Fig. 5a, HepG2 cells that cultivated with MSN-BA-SiCD showed blue fluorescence under CLSM. However, the green fluorescence of BA (Em=516 nm) was gradually emitted along with the incubation of H2O2 (Fig. 5b). On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity of blue channel for SiCD (Em=450 nm) was decreased over time due to the arising of FRET. As the time went on, the colour of merge images for samples with H2O2 were gradually changed from blue to green within 4 h, which illustrated that MSN-BA-SiCD are able to detect theindicating that the MSN-BA-SiCD nanosensors had low cytotoxicity to cells (Fig. S16). Therefore, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and visualized intracellular sensing could be realized for MSN-BA-SiCD, which reflects their potential applications in the fields of biological.

In addition, H2O2 is an important indicator in food safety. Therefore, the application of the detection performance of MSN-BA-SiCD in real samples was studied with beer and orange juice. Beer and orange juice samples with various concentrations of H2O2 were prepared to evaluate the detectability and accuracy of MSN-BA-SiCD in these drinks. The fluorescence changes of MSN-BA-SiCD in drinks caused by the addition of H2O2 were used to estimate the concentration and the results were compared with known values. As we can see from Table 1, the recoveries of beer and orange juice at six different concentration gradients are ranged from 50 μM to 300 μM and most of the relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 8%. The practicability of the MSN-BA-SiCD nanosensor for the precise sensing of H2O2 is well proven by these results.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on MSN for highly sensitive and selective H2O2 detecting was designed by using a highly water-soluble carbon dot and an AIE charactered responsive molecule BA. Larger conjugated compound could formed by the recognized cyclization reaction between BA and H2O2, which produced an intense fluorescence emission at 516 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of SiCD at 450 nm was effectively quenched by FRET. A visual ratio fluorescence sensing process for H2O2 was constructed based on the decrease of the fluorescence of SiCD and the increase of the fluorescence of BA. The naonosensor has been demonstrated to give a LOD of 4.78 μM for H2O2. The water solubility of the nanosensor has been effectively improved through the modification of SiCD, while the photostability of the nanosensor has been greatly enhanced by the protection of the silica matrix. Additionally, the intracellular visualized recognition of H2O2 could be realized by MSN-BA-SiCD using CLSM in real time. Effortless detection of H2O2 in commercial drinks has given us an available proof of concept for its practical applications in food and environmental safety. This nanosensor showed excellent biocompatibility, stability and sensing functions, which was expected to further promote the development of H2O2 sensors in the environmental and biological fields.
